original_hypothesis.title: Per-User Cap Bypass via NFT Transfer Enables Single Actor to Fill Hard Cap
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy() checks `balanceOf(recipient) == 0` to determine if a new NFT should be minted. The per-user cap is enforced against `_position.raiseAmount` of the current NFT. If a user transfers their NFT to another address, balanceOf drops to 0, and they can buy() again with a fresh position and full maxCapPerUser allocation. Repeated indefinitely until hardCap is reached.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The per-user cap is the key fairness mechanism in ILO sales. If a single entity can bypass it, they can monopolize the entire offering, violating the economic guarantee of fair distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf(recipient) == 0 check for minting; cap enforced per-position raiseAmount, not per-address cumulative; ERC721 transferable

preconditions: Whitelisted address (or whitelist bypassed per ECON-H002)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf(recipient) resets to 0 after NFT transfer; new position starts with raiseAmount=0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: buy(maxCap) → transfer NFT → buy(maxCap) → repeat until hardCap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Monopolize ILO allocation; if token appreciates post-launch, attacker captures all upside

economic_questions: What is the typical hardCap/maxCapPerUser ratio (number of iterations needed)?
Does the whitelist check on recipient block the transferred NFT's new holder from participating?
What is the gas cost of repeated buy+transfer cycles?
validation_requirements: Confirm NFT transfer resets balanceOf for original address
Confirm fresh buy() works after transfer with full cap
Test full cycle: buy → transfer → buy

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for N buy+transfer transactions (~N * 0.005 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire ILO allocation profit if token appreciates

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During sale window, before other buyers fill cap
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Other legitimate participants completely excluded from ILO

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of ILO participation opportunity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire ILO allocation minus attacker's share

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace: buy(max, A) → A.transfer(NFT, B) → buy(max, A) confirms cap bypass

validation_result.mechanism: per-user cap bypass via NFT transfer
validation_result.impact_summary: A single whitelisted user can bypass the maxCapPerUser limit by transferring their ILO NFT to another address, resetting their balanceOf to 0, and buying again with a fresh position up to the full hardCap.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ILOPool.buy() function uses `balanceOf(recipient) == 0` (line ~120) to decide whether to mint a new NFT or add to an existing position. The per-user cap is enforced against `_position.raiseAmount` of the current token (line ~127). Since ILOPool inherits from OpenZeppelin ERC721 with standard `transferFrom`/`safeTransferFrom` and does NOT override `_beforeTokenTransfer` or `_transfer` to prevent NFT transfers, a user can: (1) buy maxCapPerUser to receive NFT#1, (2) transfer NFT#1 to any other address via ERC721.transferFrom, (3) call buy() again — since balanceOf is now 0, a fresh NFT#2 is minted with raiseAmount=0, passing the cap check. This cycle repeats until totalRaised reaches hardCap. There is no per-address cumulative tracking of raise amounts.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 120-128 of ILOPool.sol clearly show the balanceOf check and per-position cap enforcement. No transfer restriction exists in the contract. The ERC721 base class provides unrestricted transferFrom. The _positions mapping is keyed by tokenId, not by address, so transferring the NFT completely disconnects the old owner from any cap tracking.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The cap check is performed against _position.raiseAmount for the current tokenId only. After transferring the NFT, balanceOf(recipient) resets to 0, causing a new NFT to be minted with a fresh position (raiseAmount=0). The cap check then allows another full maxCapPerUser allocation. There is no cumulative per-address tracking.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker (whitelisted) calls buy(maxCapPerUser, attackerAddr) — passes whitelist, time, hardcap checks
4. Attacker calls ERC721.transferFrom(attackerAddr, coldWallet, 1) — standard ERC721, no override prevents this
9. Repeat N times until totalRaised == hardCap
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists because: (a) balanceOf check at ILOPool.sol:120 uses ERC721.balanceOf which decrements on transfer (b) per-user cap at ILOPool.sol:127 is checked against _positions[tokenId].raiseAmount for the NEW tokenId which starts at 0 (c) No _beforeTokenTransfer override exists to prevent NFT transfers (d) No mapping(address => uint256) tracks cumulative raise amounts per address

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect is inherent in the buy() logic and ERC721 transferability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward: 1) Whitelisted user calls buy(maxCapPerUser, self). 2) Transfers NFT to any other address via ERC721.transferFrom. 3) Calls buy(maxCapPerUser, self) again. 4) Repeats until hardCap is filled. Each iteration costs one buy() + one transferFrom() gas cost. The attacker needs RAISE_TOKEN equal to the hardCap but monopolizes the entire ILO allocation. If using multiple wallets to receive transferred NFTs, the attacker still controls all positions.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The maxCapPerUser is the primary fairness mechanism ensuring equitable distribution among whitelisted participants. A single actor can monopolize the entire ILO by filling the hardCap, completely excluding all other legitimate participants. This violates the fundamental economic guarantee of fair distribution (INV-ECON-002 related). The attacker captures the full upside if the token appreciates post-launch.
recommended_mitigations: Add a mapping(address => uint256) to track cumulative raise amounts per address, and check against that instead of per-position raiseAmount
Override _beforeTokenTransfer in ILOPool to prevent NFT transfers before launch (or entirely during the sale period)
Consider making the NFT non-transferable (soulbound) until after launch and initial vesting cliff
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ILOPool with hardCap=10 ETH, maxCapPerUser=1 ETH. 2. Whitelist address A. 3. A calls buy(1 ETH, A) → receives NFT#1. 4. A calls transferFrom(A, B, 1) to transfer NFT#1 to B. 5. A calls buy(1 ETH, A) → receives NFT#2. 6. Assert totalRaised == 2 ETH, confirming cap bypass. 7. Repeat steps 4-6 until totalRaised == 10 ETH.
uncertainties: The whitelist check (_isWhitelisted) must pass for the recipient on each buy() call — if whitelist is strictly enforced, only whitelisted addresses can be the recipient, but the same whitelisted address can buy repeatedly after transferring their NFT
additional_testing_needed: Verify that tokenOfOwnerByIndex is available (ERC721Enumerable extension) — the code uses it at line 123, confirming ERC721 with enumeration is used
Test the full cycle with gas cost analysis to determine practical iteration limits
Verify no hidden _beforeTokenTransfer override in inherited contracts that might block transfers
